summary: A Caribbean-born Founding Father who served as George Washington's Revolutionary War aide-de-camp and became the first US Secretary of the Treasury, where he wrote the First Report on Public Credit, pushed for federal assumption of state debts, and chartered the First Bank of the United States. He co-wrote the majority of the Federalist Papers under the pseudonym 'Publius' and led the Federalist Party, but his career and reputation were damaged by the Reynolds Affair, an extramarital affair exposed by the pamphleteer James Callender. He was killed in an 1804 duel with Vice President Aaron Burr in Weehawken, New Jersey, and is the subject of Lin-Manuel Miranda's musical Hamilton.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Hamilton is identified in giveaways: a Caribbean-born Founding Father who became George Washington's Revolutionary War aide-de-camp and the first US Secretary of the Treasury, before being killed in an 1804 duel with Vice President Aaron Burr. He co-wrote the majority of the Federalist Papers and later succeeded Washington as senior officer of the US Army.

clues.clue: After George Washington's death, Hamilton succeeded him as Senior Officer of the United States Army and suggested using an army to invade Spanish colonies
examples: This man suggested using an army to invade Spanish colonies after he succeeded George Washington as Senior Officer of the United States Army.

works.name: Early Life and Rise
works.description: Born on the island of Nevis and raised on St. Croix in the Danish Virgin Islands, Hamilton came to New York City, where he lived with the Patriot spy Hercules Mulligan and attended Columbia (then King's College). He served as George Washington's aide-de-camp during the Revolutionary War, exchanged flirty letters with John Laurens that have led some historians to speculate he may have been bisexual, and married Elizabeth 'Eliza' Schuyler, daughter of the New York political and military figure Philip Schuyler.

clues.clue: Born on the island of Nevis; biographies by James T. Flexner and Ron Chernow explore his youth on St. Croix in the Danish Virgin Islands
examples: This man was born on the island of Nevis.
Biographies by James T. Flexner and Ron Chernow explore the St. Croix youth of the first Treasury Secretary.

clues.clue: Upon arriving in New York City, Hamilton lived with the Patriot spy Hercules Mulligan and attended Columbia University
examples: This man lived with Mulligan after arriving in New York City. He attended Columbia University and served as the first Secretary of the Treasury.

clues.clue: Hamilton exchanged flirty letters with fellow Continental Army officer John Laurens, leading some historians to speculate he may have been bisexual
examples: This Founding Father exchanged flirty letters with John Laurens, leading some historians to speculate he may have been bisexual. He would later become the first Secretary of the Treasury.

clues.clue: Hamilton's father-in-law Philip Schuyler, a Revolutionary War general accused of dereliction of duty by Horatio Gates, later served as a New York senator before losing his seat to Aaron Burr
examples: This father-in-law of Hamilton was accused by Horatio Gates of dereliction of duty. He later served as a senator from New York in the United States Congress, and was defeated in 1791 by Aaron Burr.
Schuyler was the father-in-law of this more prominent Federalist, who had an affair with Maria Reynolds and authored the Report on Manufactures.

examples: Even before the Reynolds Affair, Hamilton was a frequent target in the National Gazette, a paper owned and edited by this early American author who savaged early Federalists.

examples: He was called a "bastard brat of a Scotch pedlar" by John Adams, whom he battled for party control.

works.name: The Federalist Papers and Constitutional Theory
works.description: Hamilton's first published works refuted the loyalist writer Samuel Seabury before he became the primary author of the Federalist Papers, writing the first essay (unlike the tenth, written by Madison) and Federalist 84, which argued against the need for a Bill of Rights. As Treasury Secretary, he argued for a broad reading of the Constitution's 'general welfare clause' and later debated Madison over the constitutionality of Washington's Proclamation of Neutrality in the pseudonymous 'Pacificus-Helvidius' exchange.

clues.clue: Hamilton's first published works refuted the loyalist Westchester Farmer Samuel Seabury; he then wrote the first Federalist Paper, unlike the tenth, which was written by James Madison
examples: This man's first published works refuted the loyalist Westchester Farmer Samuel Seabury. This man wrote 51 Federalist Papers and served as the first Secretary of the Treasury.
Unlike the tenth Federalist paper, the first Federalist paper was written by this first Secretary of the Treasury, who was killed by Aaron Burr in a duel in Weehawken, New Jersey.

clues.clue: In Federalist 84, Hamilton argued against the need for a Bill of Rights, decrying it as an unnecessary exception to powers not explicitly granted by the Constitution
examples: Perhaps one of the most famous individual Federalist Papers is Federalist 84, written by Hamilton. In Federalist 84, Hamilton argues against the need for these, later introduced by James Madison, decrying them as unnecessary exceptions to powers not explicitly granted by the Constitution.

clues.clue: Hamilton put forth a broad interpretation of the Constitution's general welfare clause, claiming it granted Congress plenary power to spend
examples: A broad interpretation of the general welfare clause was put forth by this Founding Father, who claimed that it granted Congress plenary power to spend. He was the first US Secretary of the Treasury.

clues.clue: Hamilton and Madison conducted the pseudonymous Pacificus-Helvidius debates over Washington's Proclamation of Neutrality
examples: Hamilton and Madison later conducted the Pacificus-Helvidius debates over this policy. A later speech defended this policy by noting, "Our detached and distant situation invites and enables us to pursue a different course."

works.name: Treasury Secretary and Economic Policy
works.description: As the first Secretary of the Treasury, Hamilton wrote the First Report on Public Credit and the Report on Manufactures, pushing for the federal assumption of state debts as part of the 'Compromise of 1790' that placed the national capital in Washington, D.C. in exchange for Southern support. He also chartered the First Bank of the United States, devised a 'funding at par' scheme to bolster national credit, founded the Federalist Party and the New York Evening Post, and recommended himself to Robert Morris for the Treasury post.

clues.clue: Hamilton pushed for the federal assumption of state debts as part of an economic policy that also included the charter of the First Bank of the United States; the assumption plan was part of the 'Compromise of 1790,' under which Madison and Jefferson assented in exchange for the establishment of the federal capital in Washington, D.C.
examples: This Secretary of the Treasury under George Washington pushed for the federal assumption of state debt as part of an economic policy that included the charter of the First Bank of the United States.
Name or describe this policy that James Madison and Thomas Jefferson assented to in the Compromise of 1790 in exchange for the establishment of a federal capital within the District of Columbia.

clues.clue: Hamilton wrote the First Report on Public Credit and the Report on Manufactures
examples: That Cabinet member with this surname produced the First Report on Public Credit and advocated for a National Bank.
For 10 points, name this author of the Report on Manufactures and father of the National Bank of the United States.

clues.clue: Hamilton's 'funding at par' scheme retired old government bonds by issuing new ones matching their face value, to bolster national credit
examples: Hamilton's scheme to bolster the nation's credit was given this name. It involved taking in old bonds and issuing new ones that match the face value of the old bonds.

clues.clue: Hamilton led the Federalist Party, whose platform included creating a national bank and maintaining good relations with Britain
examples: Alexander Hamilton was a leader of this early political party, which opposed the Democratic Republicans. This party's platform included the creation of a national bank and maintaining good relations with Britain.

clues.clue: Hamilton founded the newspaper that became the New York Evening Post
examples: The newspaper was founded by this author of the Report on Manufactures and national debt advocate.

clues.clue: Robert Morris, the wartime Superintendent of Finance, turned down the offer to be the first Treasury Secretary and recommended Hamilton instead
examples: Morris was suggested to be the inaugural Secretary of the Treasury, but he turned it down and suggested this man, a leading Federalist, who got the job.

works.name: The Reynolds Affair
works.description: Hamilton's extramarital affair with Maria Reynolds, and the blackmail payments he made to her husband James Reynolds, became the first major sex scandal in US history after the pamphleteer James Callender exposed it. Hamilton responded with the 95-page pamphlet Observations on Certain Documents, admitting to the affair to refute separate corruption charges that House Speaker Frederick Muhlenberg had helped uncover.

clues.clue: Hamilton had an extramarital affair with Maria Reynolds and paid blackmail money to her husband, James Reynolds, to keep it quiet; he later told James Monroe about the affair to explain questionable expenses after being accused of improper speculation
examples: He paid James Reynolds blackmail money to temporarily avoid reports that he was having an affair with Reynolds' wife, Maria.
Hamilton was involved in the first major sex scandal in US history when his extramarital affair with this woman and the bribes paid to her husband became public knowledge.

clues.clue: The pamphleteer James Callender exposed the Reynolds Affair to the public, having previously been a favorite of Republican politicians until he later revealed Thomas Jefferson's affair with Sally Hemings
examples: The Reynolds Affair was brought to light by this pamphleteer, who would also later expose Thomas Jefferson's affair with Sally Hemings.
Callender's broadsides exposed this man's affair with Maria Reynolds.

clues.clue: Hamilton wrote the 95-page pamphlet Observations on Certain Documents to refute corruption charges (a case of alleged speculation uncovered with help from Speaker of the House Frederick Muhlenberg), admitting to and apologizing for the Reynolds affair in the process
examples: This man wrote the 95-page Observations on Certain Documents, in which he refuted corruption charges levied by James Thomson Callender.This politician was having an affair with the wife of a man speculating on unpaid back wages intended for war veterans, a scandal uncovered by Speaker of the House Frederick Muhlenberg.

works.name: The Duel with Aaron Burr
works.description: Hamilton's rivalry with Aaron Burr included his founding of the Manhattan Company bank (under the guise of a water company) to compete with Burr's Bank of New York, his role convincing Federalist James Bayard to abstain in the contested 1800 presidential election to block Burr, and Burr's defeat of Hamilton's father-in-law Philip Schuyler for a Senate seat. The rivalry culminated in an 1804 duel in Weehawken, New Jersey, in which Burr fatally shot Hamilton and was later charged with treason for allegedly conspiring with James Wilkinson.

examples: To compete with the other's (*) Bank of New York, one of these two people founded the Manhattan Company bank under the guise of establishing a shoddy water system.

clues.clue: Hamilton convinced Federalist congressman James Bayard and his colleagues to abstain from voting in the contested 1800 election to prevent Aaron Burr from being elected president; a letter by Charles Cooper claimed Hamilton had expressed a 'despicable opinion' of Burr

According to a letter by Charles D. Cooper, one of these two people expressed a "despicable opinion" about the other, prompting a failed attempt to extract an apology.

clues.clue: Aaron Burr defeated Hamilton's father-in-law Philip Schuyler in a New York Senate race

works.name: Legacy and Cultural Depictions
works.description: Hamilton is the subject of Lin-Manuel Miranda's musical Hamilton, which dramatizes episodes like the destruction of his love letters to Eliza Schuyler in the song 'Burn' and inspired the companion album The Hamilton Mixtape. He was portrayed earlier in a play co-written by Mary P. Hamlin and George Arliss, who later played him in a 1931 film, and one bonus claims a distant relation to a Scottish nobleman who also died in a pistol duel.

comprehensive_summary: Alexander Hamilton was born on the island of Nevis and raised on St. Croix in the Danish Virgin Islands (his youth there explored in biographies by James T. Flexner and Ron Chernow) before arriving in New York City, where he lived with the Patriot spy Hercules Mulligan and attended Columbia (then King's College). During the Revolutionary War he served as George Washington's aide-de-camp, exchanged flirty letters with fellow officer John Laurens that have led some historians to speculate he may have been bisexual, and married Elizabeth 'Eliza' Schuyler, daughter of the Revolutionary War general and future New York senator Philip Schuyler. His first published works refuted the loyalist Samuel Seabury, and he went on to co-write 51 of the 85 Federalist Papers under the pseudonym 'Publius' alongside James Madison and John Jay, penning the first essay (unlike the tenth, written by Madison) and Federalist 84, which argued against the need for a Bill of Rights; as Treasury Secretary he also put forth a broad interpretation of the Constitution's 'general welfare clause' and, in the pseudonymous Pacificus-Helvidius debates, defended Washington's Proclamation of Neutrality against Madison's objections. As the first Secretary of the Treasury — a post Robert Morris turned down in favor of recommending him — Hamilton wrote the First Report on Public Credit and the Report on Manufactures, pushed for the federal assumption of state debts as part of the 'Compromise of 1790' that placed the national capital in Washington, D.C. in exchange for Southern support, chartered the First Bank of the United States, devised a 'funding at par' scheme to bolster national credit, founded the Federalist Party (whose platform favored a national bank and good relations with Britain), and founded the newspaper that became the New York Evening Post; his rival John Adams, with whom he battled for party control, called him a 'bastard brat of a Scotch pedlar,' and he was targeted even before his greatest scandal by Philip Freneau's National Gazette. That scandal, the Reynolds Affair, began with an extramarital affair with Maria Reynolds and blackmail payments to her husband James Reynolds; the pamphleteer James Callender (previously a favorite of Republican politicians until he later exposed Thomas Jefferson's affair with Sally Hemings) exposed the affair to the public, prompting Hamilton to write the 95-page pamphlet Observations on Certain Documents admitting to and apologizing for it in order to refute separate corruption charges that Speaker of the House Frederick Muhlenberg had helped uncover — he later told James Monroe about the affair to explain questionable expenses after being accused of improper speculation. Hamilton's rivalry with Aaron Burr included his founding of the Manhattan Company bank (under the guise of a water system) to compete with Burr's Bank of New York, his role convincing Federalist congressman James Bayard to abstain in the contested 1800 presidential election to block Burr's path to the presidency (after Burr sought an apology over a 'despicable opinion' Hamilton had reportedly expressed, per a letter by Charles Cooper), and Burr's defeat of Philip Schuyler for a Senate seat; the rivalry culminated in their 1804 duel in Weehawken, New Jersey, where Burr's second William Van Ness hid Burr's face with an umbrella from witnesses including David Hosack, and two shots were fired before Hamilton was mortally wounded — Burr was later charged with (and acquitted of) treason for allegedly conspiring with James Wilkinson to form a western empire. Hamilton is the subject of Lin-Manuel Miranda's musical Hamilton, whose song 'Burn' depicts Eliza destroying his love letters to her after learning of the Reynolds affair and which inspired the companion album The Hamilton Mixtape; those same love letters, part of a collection curated by Harold Syrett, underwent 2018 hyperspectral analysis that revealed a crossed-out racy passage about 'unrestrained intercourses.' He had been dramatized earlier in a play co-written by Mary P. Hamlin and George Arliss, who also portrayed him in a 1931 film, and one bonus identifies him as a distant relative of the Scottish nobleman James Hamilton, 4th Duke of Hamilton, who also died in a pistol duel, at Hyde Park in 1712.
recursive_suggestions: Aaron Burr — recurring figure with substantial independent material (1800 election, treason trial, Wilkinson conspiracy); watch for overlap with this page's duel section
Maria Reynolds / the Reynolds Affair — recurring scandal with independent bonus-part presence; currently covered as part of Hamilton's page since it is clued only through him
James Callender — recurring pamphleteer figure (3x in recurring bonus answers) with independent material spanning both the Hamilton and Jefferson-Hemings scandals; category filter to History
Philip Schuyler — recurring figure (Hamilton's father-in-law) with some independent military/political material (Saratoga command, Senate career); category filter to History
Federalist Papers — recurring work with independent presence; category filter to History or Literature depending on treatment

Excluded: a tossup on 'Hamilton' that also clued the colonial physician-writer Dr. Alexander Hamilton (Tuesday Club, Itinerarium) and the lawyer Andrew Hamilton (Independence Hall, Zenger trial) was filtered to keep only the clues about this Alexander Hamilton; a bonus part primarily about James Hamilton, 4th Duke of Hamilton (a 1712 English duel) was excluded except for its passing mention of this Hamilton as a 'distant American relative'
